title: He Awa He Tangata: The Mangakōtukutuku Stream as Cultural Heartland
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu's relationship with the Mangakōtukutuku stream embodies kaitiakitanga, drawing on local pūrākau (stories) of the taniwha protector and historical accounts of mahinga kai (food gathering) practices along its banks
learningObjectives: Analyze how Ngāti Kahungunu's historical relationship with the Mangakōtukutuku stream reflects concepts of kaitiakitanga and cultural identity
Compare traditional and contemporary environmental practices through interviews with local kaumatua and environmental scientists
Create a digital story map documenting significant cultural sites along the Mangakōtukutuku stream and their social significance
Develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan addressing one current environmental challenge facing the stream

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Stream Story Gathering
activities.description: Students visit three significant sites along the Mangakōtukutuku stream with local kaumatua to record oral histories, learn traditional place names, and understand the cultural significance of specific features like the deep pool where the taniwha was said to dwell

activities.title: Then and Now: Mahinga Kai Comparison
activities.description: Using historical photographs from the local marae archive and current observations, students create comparative displays showing traditional food gathering practices versus modern usage, analyzing how social practices have evolved while maintaining cultural connections

activities.title: Digital Whakapapa Mapping
activities.description: Using ArcGIS or Google Earth, students create interactive maps labeling cultural sites in te reo Māori, embedding audio recordings of stories, and documenting environmental changes observed through water quality testing at different points along the stream

activities.title: Kaitiaki Panel Challenge
activities.description: Students present their environmental action plans to a panel comprising local iwi representatives, regional council environmental officers, and community leaders, receiving feedback on both cultural appropriateness and practical implementation

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated environmental management plans
Local kaumatua Rangi Wall and Hine Palmer for oral histories
Hawke's Bay Regional Council stream monitoring toolkit and water testing equipment
Digital archive 'Te Puna o Mangakōtukutuku' hosted by the local marae

tasks: Digital story map presentation to whānau and community
Written reflection connecting personal observations to concepts of kaitiakitanga
Group kaitiakitanga action plan proposal with implementation steps

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how cultural practices vary but reflect similar purposes
Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies
Science L4: Explore aspects of the local environment and how living things are suited to their particular habitat
